title: The Christian Parent
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Christian Parent - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 6_4.txt
outline: Negative 1: Do not provoke your children to wrath (Ephesians 6:4; Colossians 3:21) — sources of bitterness include hypocrisy, self-righteousness, and conditional love
Negative 2: Do not exasperate your children with inconsistency
Positive: Raise children in the training (paideia — what is done) and admonition (nouthesia — what is said) of the Lord
Formal teaching: family devotions, catechism, Scripture (Deuteronomy 6:6-7)
Informal teaching: throughout the day, by example, interacting about God's truth
Teaching the blessings of redemption: pointing children to Christ as the reason for obedience
Parents bear primary responsibility — church and school are secondary
Enjoy your children as a gift from God
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Ephesians 6:4 on the calling of the Christian parent, with particular attention to fathers. He begins with two negative commands — do not provoke children to anger or bitterness through hypocrisy, self-righteousness, or conditional love — before turning to the positive call to raise children in the training and admonition of the Lord. Drawing heavily on Deuteronomy 6, he shows that parents must keep God's Word constantly before their children through both formal teaching (family devotions, catechism) and informal teaching throughout daily life, always pointing children to redemption in Christ as the goal and motivation for all obedience.
transcript:
We must start this sermon in the same place we started last week — looking to Christ. We are going to be talking about parenting, and it is easy to lose sight of the foundation and goal of parenting, easy to get lost in the everyday trenches of the day-to-day obstacles, and to forget what the goal of parenting actually is: to help our children to know Jesus.

I hope we understand that though we want polite children, though we want well-educated children, though we want children who don't pick their noses — ultimately what we desire is children who follow the Lord. If they are dumb as a doornail and yet they love Jesus, that is the greatest thing in the world. If they can't use a fork to save their lives but they love Jesus — that is what we are after. And it is important to remember that that can only be accomplished by the grace of God. It is by His grace, His strength, that we can be faithful in raising our children.

When God redeemed us through the personal work of the Lord Jesus Christ, He made us new creatures. The old things have passed away; behold, all things are new. As new creatures recreated in the image of Christ, we now sit in heavenly places with Jesus (Ephesians 2:6). We are enabled by God's grace to live heavenly lives now — and that includes our family life. Because of Christ, the wife can submit to her husband. Because of Christ, the husband can love his wife. Because of Christ, a child can honor and obey his or her parents. And because of Christ, a parent will not discourage their children but rather raise them in the hope of the gospel.

This is gospel living. All living begins here and ends here, in the hope of the gospel.

If you are in a place where your children are not doing well and you feel like a failure as a parent, you are in good company — most parents at one point or another have felt that way. But God is gracious, and He forgives, and He restores, and He enables us to follow Him regardless of where we are.

## Two Negatives Before the Positive

I want to begin with two negatives before we consider the positive of parenting. Our primary text is Ephesians 6:4.

I want you to notice that in this text the *fathers* are specifically addressed. That is also true of Colossians 3:21, where Paul says, "Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged." It is true that the word *fathers* could be generally applied to parents. However, in this context I believe Paul means fathers specifically, because in the very previous verse he used the general word for parents. Here in verse 4 he switches to the specific word for *father*. Paul singles out fathers because we are more likely to provoke our children — that seems to be something of a universal truth.

### Negative 1: Do Not Make Your Children Bitter

The first negative: do not give reasons for your children to become bitter toward God or toward you by the way that you parent them.

Even though our English translation uses "do not provoke" in both Ephesians and Colossians, those phrases actually translate two different Greek words. In Ephesians the word means *make angry*. In Colossians the more accurate translation is *do not embitter your children*.

What things could cause our children to be embittered?

**Hypocrisy** is a great source of bitterness. If we as fathers are living a hypocritical life — expecting something from our children but not expecting it from ourselves — we are sowing bitterness in their hearts.

**Self-righteousness** is a great source of bitterness. When we consider ourselves the greatest thing since sliced bread, when we think our children are just blessed to have us as their parents, that self-righteousness will breed bitterness.

**Conditional love** is a great source of bitterness. Let me read a quote from New Testament scholar N.T. Wright that explains what conditional love looks like in parenting. He says: "Obedience must never be made the condition of parental love. A love so conditioned would not deserve the name." If your love for your children is based on how well they obey you, how well they behave, how well they conform to whatever norms — good or bad — that you have for them, that is not true love. That is idolatry on your part, placing yourself as God in their lives. Christ loves us because He loves us, not because of any performance on our side.

So this first negative is: do not parent in such a way that will engender bitterness toward you as their parent and toward the Lord.

### Negative 2: Do Not Exasperate Your Children

The second negative: do not make your children angry by the way that you parent them. They might be angry at your right doing, and that is fine — that is what discipline is for. But do not cause them to be angry unnecessarily.

Having a moving target for obedience frustrates children. Today this is fine, but tomorrow it is not. Today they get away with everything, but tomorrow if they blink wrongly, the wrath of God falls on them. That inconsistency exasperates children.

These two negatives discourage children from obedience and by implication discourage them from obeying the Lord.

## The Positive: Raising Children in the Training and Admonition of the Lord

Now for the positive — and we will spend most of our time here. The Christian parent raises his children in the training and admonition of the Lord. Our goal in parenting is that our children know Jesus. That is our angle. That is the target.

Ephesians 6:4: "And you, fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord."

**Bring them up** — this is the idea of nourishment, of feeding them what they need to grow up in the Lord. Psalm 128 speaks of our children as little olive plants around our table, being nourished, given what they need. As parents, specifically as fathers, we need to feed our children what they need to grow in the Lord.

Interestingly, the only other time this word for nourishment appears in Ephesians is in 5:29, where it describes the way that Christ nourishes His church. Father, that is your standard: nourish your children the same way that Christ nourishes His church. That is our calling.

This bringing them up happens in two specific ways:

### Training: What Is Done

The word *training* (*paideia*) refers primarily to what is *done* to the child — training by hands-on example, by structure, by modeling, by discipline. God has given parents this tool of discipline so that our children can grow to be more like Jesus. There is a mystical union between the rod and the heart of children that helps them follow the Lord. Counterintuitive as it may seem, the Bible tells us that we hate our children if we withhold this instrument of grace from them. Hebrews 12 says that a father who does not discipline his children is a father who does not love his children. And the argument of Hebrews 12 is that you can look to the love of your earthly father and thereby know something of the love of your heavenly Father. How much we fail our children if we are not doing the things and saying the things and wielding the rod as God calls us to do.

This training involves training in righteousness — and the Lord gets to tell us what to do, because it is *the training of the Lord*.

### Admonition: What Is Said

The word *admonition* (*nouthesia*) refers primarily to what is *said* to the child. It is a word made of two parts meaning "to set the mind straight." In parenting, we set the minds of our children straight.

What is said and what is done must match, or children will be provoked to wrath and parents will be the object of God's displeasure.

In order to admonish our children — to set their minds straight — we need to keep God's Word constantly before them. Do you believe that the Word of God is powerful, sharper than any two-edged sword, dividing between bone and marrow, exposing all that is in the heart of man? Do you believe that the Word of God equips us to be complete in Christ in every situation? Then we must keep that Word before our children at every moment.

## Keeping God's Word Before Our Children (Deuteronomy 6)

The book of Deuteronomy is outlined to match the Ten Commandments. Chapter 5 introduces the commandments. Chapter 6 through 12 discusses the application of the first commandment — how not to have any god before the true God of the Bible. And the very first thing Moses addresses is how the Word of God should be before our children as a fulfillment of that command.

> *"Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one. You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might."* (Deuteronomy 6:4–5)

This is the summary of the entire Bible. And the very next thing God says is: how do you execute that command?

> *"And these words that I command you today shall be on your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children."* (Deuteronomy 6:6–7)

Do you want to obey the first commandment? Keep the Word of God in your heart. Teach the Word of God to your children.

### Formal Teaching

Deuteronomy 6:7 speaks of formal teaching: "you shall talk of them when you sit in your house." This is primarily accomplished through family devotions. Whatever age your children are, you should be having family devotions with them.

Family devotions do not have to be long or complicated. They can be brief. You don't want to provoke them to wrath by extending it beyond what they can hold. They should be interesting, at the children's level, interactive, and creative. You can simply read a verse or two of Scripture and talk about it. You can sing a hymn. You can work through a question from the Shorter Catechism or a section of the Heidelberg Catechism. You can use the curriculum your Sunday school teachers are using and reinforce it at home.

Proverbs is a great model for parental instruction. After dinner is often a great time — everyone is around the table, the younger ones are strapped in, and no one can run away.

Pray with your children. Simple prayers, a few requests. Children have tender hearts toward prayer — take advantage of that.

Parenting is not accidental. It requires being deliberate and diligent.

### Informal Teaching

But it does not stop there. Deuteronomy 6:7 continues: "and shall talk of them when you walk by the way, when you lie down, and when you rise up." Not just formal settings, but throughout the day, as opportunity arises.

Fill your home with reminders of God's Word. As one pastor says, make sure there is a "smell of Christ" in your home — that the love of Christ is so present in the atmosphere that people walking through your house sense it.

Take advantage of the opportunity to apply God's Word to life as it happens. But do that for yourself as well — if you are only telling your children how to behave according to God's Word but not applying it to your own life, you are provoking them to bitterness and wrath.

Interact with your children about God's truth. Don't dodge their questions. Deuteronomy 6:20–21 says, "When your son asks you in time to come, 'What is the meaning of the testimonies and the statutes and the judgments which the LORD our God has commanded you?' then you shall say to your son, 'We were slaves of Pharaoh in Egypt, and the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand.'" How many times have we brushed aside little questions because it was not the convenient time, because we were too tired? And yet God sends those opportunities.

As your children get older, you have less and less control over when those conversations happen. Take every one you can get. In our home, our teenager would come in just as we were about to turn out the lights and plop into the chair in our room — and we knew what that meant. We propped ourselves up in bed and made the time. Take those opportunities to bring the Word of God to bear.

Informal teaching includes teaching **by example**. Paul says something that is both frightening and calling in Philippians 4:9: "The things which you learned and received and heard and saw in me, these do, and the God of peace will be with you." The standard is: *imitate me as I imitate Christ*. People should be able to see Christ in us, not in a hypocritical way but in a way that is true and worthy of imitation.

Be careful, however, that not every moment becomes a sermon. You don't have to preach 55 minutes every time your children ask a question. We are looking for the Word of God to be present in our teaching — not for a formal homily at every dinner.

### Teaching the Blessings of Redemption

Every opportunity you have, teach your children about the blessings of redemption. Deuteronomy 6:20–21: *Why do we do these things?* "Because we were slaves, and the LORD brought us out." That is parenting at its finest — pointing our children to the redemption we have in Jesus Christ. Why do we do things the way we do? Because Christ redeemed us. And that is where the hope is, because in the redemption we have in Christ we also have forgiveness for our failures.

## You Are Responsible

Parents, you are responsible before God to train your children. Ephesians 6:4 and Deuteronomy 6:7 both say: *you*. You cannot ultimately delegate the training of your children to anyone else. You cannot say the teacher said this, or the Sunday school teacher said that — that will not excuse you before God. It did not work for Adam, and it will not work for you.

It is not the government's job to teach your children. Enrolling them in a Christian school or homeschooling them does not fulfill your duty to train them — it is only part of it. The church's role, as important as it is, amounts to an hour and a half a week. From kindergarten through 12th grade, your children will be in any school setting only about 10% of their time. The other 90% — who are they with? You, or the influences you have allowed into their lives.

Husbands and fathers are particularly called to be spiritual leaders in the home. You are responsible for the influences in your children's lives. Whatever school choice you make, you are responsible for what your children are being taught. Psalm 1:1 says blessed is the man who does not walk in the counsel of the ungodly, does not stand in the path of sinners, does not sit in the seat of the scornful — and we need to guide our children in those same paths.

Don't be naive. All of us, including our children, are capable of every sin. As one theologian said, the seed of every sin dwells in our hearts. What sort of accountability are you providing for your children — especially regarding their phones and the entertainment they consume? First Corinthians 15:33 says, "Do not be deceived: bad company corrupts good character."

What if there is no Christian father — what about the single mom? James 1:27 answers that: pure and undefiled religion is to visit orphans and widows in their trouble. If there is no Christian father, we step in. We help. We become that father influence. Titus 2 speaks of this cross-generational influence where older men train younger men and older women train younger women.

## The Goal: Taking Our Children to Jesus

Parents, your goal in training your children is that they might see Christ. Andrew — not this Andrew, but the apostle — is famous for always bringing people to Jesus. He meets Jesus and immediately runs to his brother Peter: "Come see Him." In John 12, when some Greeks came asking about Jesus, Andrew said: I will do better than answer your questions, I will take you to Him. That is our goal as parents: taking our children to Jesus.

To do that, we need to know Him. We need to know the Scriptures. And we need to know Him as our Lord and Savior.

We tend to take Proverbs 22:6 as a promise: "Train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it." But it is not a promise — it is a statement of general truth. The way we train our children will likely be the way they follow as they grow old.

In addition to disciplining and training your children, we are called to **enjoy** our children. Psalm 127:3–5 tells us children are a heritage from the Lord, a reward, a blessing. Every good and perfect gift comes from above. Psalm 128 speaks of our children as little olive plants all around our table, to be enjoyed. In the raising of your children, enjoy them.

## Conclusion

Parents, it is your duty before God to do whatever you can to influence your children in the gospel. That is your calling. I am not a prophet, but as a parent, I know what God's will is for you: to raise your children in the nurture, training, and admonition of the Lord.

Some of you may be saying: I have failed miserably. I have missed the opportunities. My children are grown or going their own way. Remember: no one is so set in their ways that God cannot change them. We have seen that in our own church.

If that is where you are, rejoice in your failure — it is the best place to be in order to receive the grace of God. Parenting, like everything else in life, can only be done by losing your life in Jesus Christ. In our weakness, His grace is magnified and we are made strong in Him. Simply repent, open your eyes to the grace of God, and continue to nurture and admonish your children as best you can — even if your children are 50 years old, continue to nurture and pray for them by the grace of God.
